perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall (i) use reasonable efforts to maintain the confidentiality of the information and materials, whether oral, written or in any form whatsoever, of the other that may be reasonably understood, from legend s, the nature of such information itself and/or the circumstances of such information’s disclosure, to be confidential and/or proprietary thereto or to third parties to which either of them owes a duty of nondisclosure (collectively, “Confidential Informat ion”); (ii) take reasonable action in connection therewith, including without limitation at least the action that each takes to protect the confidentiality of its comparable proprietary assets; (iii) to the extent within their respective possession and/or control, upon termination of this Agreement for any reason, immediately return to the provider thereof all Confidential Information not licensed or authorized to be used or enjoyed after termination or expiration hereof, and (iv) with respect to any perso n to which disclosure is contemplated, require such person to execute an agreement providing for the treatment of Confidential Information set forth in clauses (i) through (iii).
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall (i) use commercially unreasonable efforts to maintain the confidentiality of the information and materials, whether oral, written or in any form whatsoever, of the other that may be reasonably understood, from legends, the nature of such information itself and/or the circumstances of such information’s disclosure, to be confidential and/or proprietary thereto or to third parties to which either of them owes a duty of nondisclosure (collectively, “Confidential Information”); (ii) take commercially unreasonable action in connection therewith, including without limitation at least the action that each takes to protect the confidentiality of its comparable proprietary assets; (iii) to the extent within their respective possession and/or control, upon termination of this Agreement for any reason, immediately return to the provider thereof all Confidential Information not licensed or authorized to be used or enjoyed after termination or expiration hereof, and (iv) with respect to any person to which disclosure is contemplated, require such person to execute an agreement providing for the treatment of Confidential Information set forth in clauses (i) through (iii).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable efforts' to 'commercially unreasonable efforts' creates a contradiction. Contracts often require 'reasonable efforts' and this is generally understood and defined by law. 'Commercially unreasonable efforts' is an oxymoron and undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires efforts that are reasonable to maintain secrecy. Stipulating 'commercially unreasonable' is contradictory to this legal standard for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain confidentiality. The changed text requires 'commercially unreasonable efforts'. The Perturbation Explanation notes that 'Contracts often require 'reasonable efforts' and this is generally understood and defined by law. 'Commercially unreasonable efforts' is an oxymoron and undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.' The Law Explanation specifies that 'The UTSA requires efforts that are reasonable to maintain secrecy. Stipulating 'commercially unreasonable' is contradictory to this legal standard for trade secret protection.' While there are no valid scraped law snippets available, the explanations provided make it clear that requiring 'commercially unreasonable efforts' is contradictory to the legal standard of requiring 'reasonable efforts'. The change undermines the purpose of the clause, as it would not hold up to legal scrutiny, thus creating a legal contradiction based on misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing Party, and any documents containing or reflecting the Confidential Information, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to the disclosing Party upon written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Party’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information delivered pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing Party, and any documents containing or reflecting the Confidential Information, and all copies thereof, shall be returned to the disclosing Party five years after written request, or destroyed at the disclosing Party’s option.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly returned' to 'returned five years after written request' makes the contract less enforceable. The term 'promptly' has a legal understanding based on the circumstances. Delaying it by five years is unreasonable and contradicts the need to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement outlines remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes prompt return of confidential information. Delaying the return for five years would be considered a failure to mitigate damages.
perturbation.justification: The original contract states that confidential information should be 'promptly returned'. The changed text modifies this to 'returned five years after written request'. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that 'promptly' has a legal understanding based on circumstances, and delaying it by five years is unreasonable. The Law Explanation mentions remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, including prompt return of confidential information. The scraped law snippet is unreadable, but it doesn't negate the other evidence suggesting a contradiction. The change clearly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information promptly, making it less enforceable, which contradicts the aim to mitigate damages from misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: As a violation by either Party of this Agreement could cause irreparable injury to the other Party and as there is no adequate remedy at law for such violation, the non -breaching Party may, in additi on to any other remedies available to it at law or in equity, enjoin the breaching Party in a court of equity for violating or threatening to violate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: As a violation by either Party of this Agreement could cause reparable injury to the other Party and as there is an adequate remedy at law for such violation, the non-breaching Party may, in addition to any other remedies available to it at law or in equity, enjoin the breaching Party in a court of equity for violating or threatening to violate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irreparable injury' to 'reparable injury' and 'no adequate remedy at law' to 'an adequate remedy at law' directly contradicts the justification for seeking injunctive relief. Injunctive relief is typically granted when monetary damages are insufficient, so this change makes it harder to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically Rule 65 concerning injunctions, requires demonstrating a likelihood of irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted. This modification claims harm can be repaired.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established legal principles regarding injunctive relief. Injunctive relief, as supported by the Law Explanation referencing Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 65, requires demonstrating the likelihood of irreparable harm. By changing 'irreparable injury' to 'reparable injury' and 'no adequate remedy at law' to 'an adequate remedy at law', the modified text undermines the very basis for seeking injunctive relief, creating a direct conflict with the conditions under which such relief is typically granted, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports the idea that immediate and irreparable injury is required for injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korea as the governing law introduces a significant challenge to the contract's enforceability, as North Korean law is generally inaccessible and inconsistent with standard commercial practices in the United States. The parties have no real relationship with North Korea, which could render the clause unenforceable as against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that the law chosen by the parties will be applied, unless there is no reasonable basis for their choice, or if application of that law would violate a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest. Selecting North Korean law when there is no connection violates the fundamental public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Principle VII.3.1 - Limits to claims for damages | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1672 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Prof. Dr. Klaus Peter Berger, LL.M. transnational law transnational law (lex mercatoria or international business law) and "Limits to claims for damages" 2016-03-01 13:58:16 https://www.trans-lex.org/img/logo_ball.png Document-Id: 948000, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/948000" Principle No. VII.3.1 - Limits to claims for damages Damages may not exceed the actual loss and are available only for loss which is proven by the aggrieved party, or where the amount of damages cannot be established with a sufficient degree of certainty, assessed by the court or tribunal. Commentary References Contract Clauses Commentary 1 The Principle sets limits to the Principle of full compensation . Such compensation is always limited to the loss suffered by the aggrieved party. Such loss may include future damages such as lost profits or further damages such as lost business chances, provided that such loss can be established with a sufficient degree of certainty. However, there are no punitive damages in transnational law and the aggrieved party may not be enriched through the damages which it claims from the party in breach. 2 Immaterial of the nature of the damage, any damage claim is subject to the general rules of burden of proof. This means that the aggrieved party must always prove the damages which it claims. 3 In special cases in which that party finds it impossible to quantify its damages, those damages may be quantified by the court or arbitral tribunal which enjoy a certain degree of discretion. perturbation.justification: The modified text selects North Korean law as the governing law. The Law Explanation argues this violates public policy, supported by Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws. While the snippets don't explicitly prohibit choosing North Korean law, Snippet 1 discusses conflict of laws, implying a court must determine which jurisdiction's law applies. Choosing North Korean law where there is no connection to the parties or transaction contradicts the principles of conflict of laws and could render the contract unenforceable, based on the Perturbation and Law explanations.
